title: Bladder Cancer
oneLiner: Urothelial carcinoma affecting older adults (peak 70-80s), strong smoking link; presents with painless visible haematuria – missed diagnosis leads to advanced stage.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Clot retention → acute kidney injury (AKI) & bladder rupture**  
  *Single finding:* Inability to pass urine + suprapubic mass + palpable bladder (percussible dullness). Catheterisation yields clots – may require 3-way catheter with irrigation.
- **Massive haematuria → hypovolaemic shock**  
  *Single finding:* Visible blood per urethra with hypotension (SBP <90) / tachycardia, not responding to analgesia. Haematocrit may drop acutely.
- **Bilateral ureteric obstruction → anuric AKI**  
  *Single finding:* Anuria (<100 mL/day) + flank tenderness **without** palpable bladder – i.e., not simply retention. Suspect muscle-invasive tumour compressing both ureters.
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - **Painless visible haematuria** (most common) – intermittent or persistent
  - Microscopic haematuria (asymptomatic or on dip)
  - Irritative LUTS: frequency, urgency, dysuria (especially if CIS [carcinoma in situ])
  - Loin pain (ureteric obstruction) / lower back or bone pain (metastases)
- Key exam findings:
  - Usually normal in early disease
  - Palpable bladder (if retention), suprapubic tenderness
  - Hard, fixed mass on bimanual examination (late, muscle-invasive)
  - Signs of anaemia, cachexia, or lymphadenopathy (supraclavicular, inguinal) if metastatic
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **UTI** – haematuria + dysuria +/- fever; urine culture positive; FBC: raised WCC/CRP
- **Prostate cancer / BPH** – hesitancy, poor stream, nocturia; DRE abnormal in prostate cancer; PSA elevated. **Splitter:** No visible haematuria – tumour is prostatic, not intravesical
- **Stones (ureteric/renal)** – colicky flank pain radiating to groin, microscopic haematuria, imaging shows calculus. **Splitter:** Rigid, non-colicky pain + visible haematuria points to bladder tumour, not stone
- **Cystitis (interstitial or infective)** – dysuria, frequency, but urine microscopy typically shows pyuria ± bacteria; culture negative in interstitial cystitis
- **Renal cell carcinoma** – loin pain, palpable mass, haematuria; imaging shows renal lesion, not bladder
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine dipstick (blood, nitrites, leucocytes) – if negative but visible haematuria persists, do not reassure
  - Bladder scan (post-void residual) – assess for retention/clot burden
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia, thrombocytopenia with bleeding), U&E/eGFR (AKI from obstruction), CRP, clotting screen (if haemorrhage or planned intervention), LFT (liver metastases)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Renal/bladder ultrasound (assess hydronephrosis, bladder mass, stones; poor for small tumours)
  - **Gold standard (visible haematuria):** CT urogram (CTU) – delayed phase to visualise upper tracts and bladder; also assess nodal/distant disease
  - **Second-line:** MRI urogram (if CT contrast contraindicated) or IVU
- **Special tests:**
  - **Flexible cystoscopy** – outpatient visualisation of bladder; biopsy/cytology
  - **Urine cytology** – high specificity for high-grade/CIS; low sensitivity for low-grade
  - **Ureteroscopy** if CT shows upper tract filling defect
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**
  - NICE guideline (NG12) – visible haematuria in anyone >45 years requires urgent (2-week wait) referral for cystoscopy + imaging
  - **Risk stratification** for non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer: EORTC risk tables (low/intermediate/high recurrence and progression) – use in MDT
- **Never-miss test:** **Urine culture** – must exclude UTI before assuming malignancy; a positive culture should be treated and then haematuria re-checked
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Active haematuria without clot retention:** IV fluids (normal saline 500 mL bolus if hypotensive), cross-match 2-4 units if Hb <80 / ongoing bleeding; alert urology on-call
  - **Clot retention:** 3-way urethral catheter (22 Fr minimum) with continuous bladder irrigation (20-30 mL/min normal saline); measure urine output hourly; bladder washout if clots block catheter
  - **Suspected obstructive AKI:** urgent renal ultrasound (bilateral hydronephrosis); if anuric → urology for nephrostomy or DJ stent
  - **Symptomatic anaemia:** pack cell transfusion (1-2 units) if Hb <70 or ongoing haemorrhage
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit all with visible haematuria requiring irrigation / haemodynamic instability
  - **If non-urgent:** schedule 2-week wait clinic for flexible cystoscopy + CT urogram if not done
  - **Inpatient workup:** urine cytology × 3 if atypia; check calcium, LDH, LFT if metastatic suspected
  - **Pain management:** regular paracetamol + NSAIDs (avoid NSAIDs if AKI or bleeding risk) +/- codeine; escalate to morphine if severe
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Non-muscle invasive (Ta/T1/CIS):** TURBT (transurethral resection) + intravesical therapy (mitomycin C for intermediate/high risk; BCG for high-risk/CIS)
  - **Muscle-invasive (≥T2):** radical cystectomy + neoadjuvant chemotherapy (cisplatin-based) or radical radiotherapy with radiosensitiser (NICE recommends discussion in MDT)
  - **Metastatic:** platinum-based chemotherapy (gemcitabine + cisplatin) / immunotherapy (pembrolizumab) second-line – per local protocol/senior
  - **Surveillance:** flexible cystoscopy every 3 months for first 2 years (non-muscle invasive), then extended intervals
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride 1 mL/kg/h if no heart failure; increase if bleeding
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (unless active bleeding/high haemorrhage risk – discuss with urology)
  - **NBM** if planned for TURBT or cystoscopy under GA (nil solids 6h, clear fluids 2h)
  - **Observations:** NEWS 2 hourly if unstable; otherwise 4-hourly
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (intravesical):**
  - **Mitomycin C:** 40 mg in 50 mL normal saline, instilled via catheter, retained for 1 hour (patient rotated every 15 min), then drained. Regimen: single dose post-TURBT; then for intermediate-risk typically 6 weekly doses. *Confirm dose with local urology pharmacy.*
  - **BCG (Bacille Calmette-Guérin):** for high-risk non-muscle invasive. Induction: 6 weekly instillations; maintenance: 3 weekly doses at 3, 6, 12 months. Dose per vial – [check local policy / BNF]
- **Common alternatives (chemotherapy):**
  - **Cisplatin** + gemcitabine for advanced disease: cisplatin 70 mg/m² IV day 1, gemcitabine 1000 mg/m² days 1, 8 – every 21 days. *Dose adjustment per creatinine clearance – [check local oncology protocol]*
  - **Carboplatin** if poor renal function (GFR <60) – dose per AUC.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - BCG: contraindicated in immunocompromised (anti-TNF, steroids, HIV), pregnancy, active TB
  - Mitomycin: avoid if known allergy, active urinary tract infection; do not use if bladder perforation suspected
  - Cisplatin: nephrotoxic – ensure adequate hydration (1 L normal saline before + 8h after); contraindicated if eGFR <60 unless carboplatin substituted
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Cisplatin – dose reduce if CrCl <60; omit if <30 (substitute carboplatin). Check eGFR before each cycle.
  - Carboplatin – dose based on Calvert formula (target AUC, e.g., 4-6)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Cisplatin without pre-hydration** – leads to acute kidney injury; always check eGFR and give at least 1 L NS before infusion; also ensure antiemetic (aprepitant + dexamethasone) prescribed
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Haemorrhage** – clot retention, hypovolaemia
  - **Catheter-related** – UTI, sepsis
  - **TURBT-related** – bladder perforation (extravasation), TUR syndrome (rare – hyponatraemia from glycine)
  - **Chemotherapy-related** – myelosuppression (neutropenic sepsis), nephrotoxicity (cisplatin), haemorrhagic cystitis
- **Chronic:**
  - **Recurrence** – up to 70% of non-muscle invasive recur within 5 years
  - **Progression** to muscle-invasive disease (10-30%)
  - **Incontinence** post-cystectomy (neobladder or ileal conduit)
  - **Ureteric stricture** after ureteroscopy or surgery
sections.Prognosis:
- **Non-muscle invasive (Ta, T1):** 5-year survival ~70-90% if treated; high recurrence rate, but progression to muscle-invasive only ~15-20%
- **Muscle-invasive (T2-4):** 5-year survival ~40-60% after curative therapy (cystectomy + chemo); metastatic disease median survival ~14-15 months
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always send urine for culture before treating haematuria as UTI** – if culture negative, consider tumour and refer on 2-week wait pathway
- **Offer smoking cessation** – single strongest modifiable risk factor; provide referral to local stop-smoking service
- **Use a 3-way catheter early** if any clot retention – 2-way catheters block quickly; 22 Fr or larger, irrigation at 20-30 mL/min
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not prescribe NSAIDs** (ibuprofen, diclofenac) for pain in patient with visible haematuria – risk of worsening bleeding and precipitating AKI
- **Do not discharge a patient with visible haematuria** without arranging urgent referral (if >45 years) – even if urine culture positive, treat first then re-check haematuria
- **Do not omit VTE prophylaxis** in admitted patient – but hold if active uncontrolled haemorrhage (discuss with urology). Resume once bleeding controlled
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Visible haematuria worsens or becomes heavy (clots, unable to void)
  - New inability to pass urine / suprapubic pain
  - Fever + rigors (suggests UTI or sepsis)
- **Follow-up:**
  - 2-week wait urology clinic if not yet seen
  - After TURBT: clinical nurse specialist phone follow-up within 48h; flexi cystoscopy 3 months
  - For metastases: oncology clinic within 2 weeks
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call urology registrar if:**
  - Clot retention not clearing with continuous irrigation
  - Haematuria requiring >2 units PRBC / ongoing haemodynamic instability
  - Severe suprapubic pain (suspect perforation)
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥ 6 or single parameter: SBP <90 / HR >120 / RR >25 / SpO2 <90% or GCS ≤13
  - Lactate > 4 mmol/L, anuria > 6 hours despite fluids
  - Anticipated massive transfusion (activate major haemorrhage protocol)

rawMarkdown:
## Bladder Cancer
**One-liner:** Urothelial carcinoma affecting older adults (peak 70-80s), strong smoking link; presents with painless visible haematuria – missed diagnosis leads to advanced stage.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Clot retention → acute kidney injury (AKI) & bladder rupture**  
  *Single finding:* Inability to pass urine + suprapubic mass + palpable bladder (percussible dullness). Catheterisation yields clots – may require 3-way catheter with irrigation.
- **Massive haematuria → hypovolaemic shock**  
  *Single finding:* Visible blood per urethra with hypotension (SBP <90) / tachycardia, not responding to analgesia. Haematocrit may drop acutely.
- **Bilateral ureteric obstruction → anuric AKI**  
  *Single finding:* Anuria (<100 mL/day) + flank tenderness **without** palpable bladder – i.e., not simply retention. Suspect muscle-invasive tumour compressing both ureters.

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - **Painless visible haematuria** (most common) – intermittent or persistent
  - Microscopic haematuria (asymptomatic or on dip)
  - Irritative LUTS: frequency, urgency, dysuria (especially if CIS [carcinoma in situ])
  - Loin pain (ureteric obstruction) / lower back or bone pain (metastases)
- Key exam findings:
  - Usually normal in early disease
  - Palpable bladder (if retention), suprapubic tenderness
  - Hard, fixed mass on bimanual examination (late, muscle-invasive)
  - Signs of anaemia, cachexia, or lymphadenopathy (supraclavicular, inguinal) if metastatic

### Differential Diagnosis
- **UTI** – haematuria + dysuria +/- fever; urine culture positive; FBC: raised WCC/CRP
- **Prostate cancer / BPH** – hesitancy, poor stream, nocturia; DRE abnormal in prostate cancer; PSA elevated. **Splitter:** No visible haematuria – tumour is prostatic, not intravesical
- **Stones (ureteric/renal)** – colicky flank pain radiating to groin, microscopic haematuria, imaging shows calculus. **Splitter:** Rigid, non-colicky pain + visible haematuria points to bladder tumour, not stone
- **Cystitis (interstitial or infective)** – dysuria, frequency, but urine microscopy typically shows pyuria ± bacteria; culture negative in interstitial cystitis
- **Renal cell carcinoma** – loin pain, palpable mass, haematuria; imaging shows renal lesion, not bladder

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine dipstick (blood, nitrites, leucocytes) – if negative but visible haematuria persists, do not reassure
  - Bladder scan (post-void residual) – assess for retention/clot burden
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia, thrombocytopenia with bleeding), U&E/eGFR (AKI from obstruction), CRP, clotting screen (if haemorrhage or planned intervention), LFT (liver metastases)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Renal/bladder ultrasound (assess hydronephrosis, bladder mass, stones; poor for small tumours)
  - **Gold standard (visible haematuria):** CT urogram (CTU) – delayed phase to visualise upper tracts and bladder; also assess nodal/distant disease
  - **Second-line:** MRI urogram (if CT contrast contraindicated) or IVU
- **Special tests:**
  - **Flexible cystoscopy** – outpatient visualisation of bladder; biopsy/cytology
  - **Urine cytology** – high specificity for high-grade/CIS; low sensitivity for low-grade
  - **Ureteroscopy** if CT shows upper tract filling defect
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**
  - NICE guideline (NG12) – visible haematuria in anyone >45 years requires urgent (2-week wait) referral for cystoscopy + imaging
  - **Risk stratification** for non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer: EORTC risk tables (low/intermediate/high recurrence and progression) – use in MDT
- **Never-miss test:** **Urine culture** – must exclude UTI before assuming malignancy; a positive culture should be treated and then haematuria re-checked

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Active haematuria without clot retention:** IV fluids (normal saline 500 mL bolus if hypotensive), cross-match 2-4 units if Hb <80 / ongoing bleeding; alert urology on-call
  - **Clot retention:** 3-way urethral catheter (22 Fr minimum) with continuous bladder irrigation (20-30 mL/min normal saline); measure urine output hourly; bladder washout if clots block catheter
  - **Suspected obstructive AKI:** urgent renal ultrasound (bilateral hydronephrosis); if anuric → urology for nephrostomy or DJ stent
  - **Symptomatic anaemia:** pack cell transfusion (1-2 units) if Hb <70 or ongoing haemorrhage
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Admit all with visible haematuria requiring irrigation / haemodynamic instability
  - **If non-urgent:** schedule 2-week wait clinic for flexible cystoscopy + CT urogram if not done
  - **Inpatient workup:** urine cytology × 3 if atypia; check calcium, LDH, LFT if metastatic suspected
  - **Pain management:** regular paracetamol + NSAIDs (avoid NSAIDs if AKI or bleeding risk) +/- codeine; escalate to morphine if severe
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Non-muscle invasive (Ta/T1/CIS):** TURBT (transurethral resection) + intravesical therapy (mitomycin C for intermediate/high risk; BCG for high-risk/CIS)
  - **Muscle-invasive (≥T2):** radical cystectomy + neoadjuvant chemotherapy (cisplatin-based) or radical radiotherapy with radiosensitiser (NICE recommends discussion in MDT)
  - **Metastatic:** platinum-based chemotherapy (gemcitabine + cisplatin) / immunotherapy (pembrolizumab) second-line – per local protocol/senior
  - **Surveillance:** flexible cystoscopy every 3 months for first 2 years (non-muscle invasive), then extended intervals
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride 1 mL/kg/h if no heart failure; increase if bleeding
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (unless active bleeding/high haemorrhage risk – discuss with urology)
  - **NBM** if planned for TURBT or cystoscopy under GA (nil solids 6h, clear fluids 2h)
  - **Observations:** NEWS 2 hourly if unstable; otherwise 4-hourly

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (intravesical):**
  - **Mitomycin C:** 40 mg in 50 mL normal saline, instilled via catheter, retained for 1 hour (patient rotated every 15 min), then drained. Regimen: single dose post-TURBT; then for intermediate-risk typically 6 weekly doses. *Confirm dose with local urology pharmacy.*
  - **BCG (Bacille Calmette-Guérin):** for high-risk non-muscle invasive. Induction: 6 weekly instillations; maintenance: 3 weekly doses at 3, 6, 12 months. Dose per vial – [check local policy / BNF]
- **Common alternatives (chemotherapy):**
  - **Cisplatin** + gemcitabine for advanced disease: cisplatin 70 mg/m² IV day 1, gemcitabine 1000 mg/m² days 1, 8 – every 21 days. *Dose adjustment per creatinine clearance – [check local oncology protocol]*
  - **Carboplatin** if poor renal function (GFR <60) – dose per AUC.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - BCG: contraindicated in immunocompromised (anti-TNF, steroids, HIV), pregnancy, active TB
  - Mitomycin: avoid if known allergy, active urinary tract infection; do not use if bladder perforation suspected
  - Cisplatin: nephrotoxic – ensure adequate hydration (1 L normal saline before + 8h after); contraindicated if eGFR <60 unless carboplatin substituted
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Cisplatin – dose reduce if CrCl <60; omit if <30 (substitute carboplatin). Check eGFR before each cycle.
  - Carboplatin – dose based on Calvert formula (target AUC, e.g., 4-6)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Cisplatin without pre-hydration** – leads to acute kidney injury; always check eGFR and give at least 1 L NS before infusion; also ensure antiemetic (aprepitant + dexamethasone) prescribed

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Haemorrhage** – clot retention, hypovolaemia
  - **Catheter-related** – UTI, sepsis
  - **TURBT-related** – bladder perforation (extravasation), TUR syndrome (rare – hyponatraemia from glycine)
  - **Chemotherapy-related** – myelosuppression (neutropenic sepsis), nephrotoxicity (cisplatin), haemorrhagic cystitis
- **Chronic:**
  - **Recurrence** – up to 70% of non-muscle invasive recur within 5 years
  - **Progression** to muscle-invasive disease (10-30%)
  - **Incontinence** post-cystectomy (neobladder or ileal conduit)
  - **Ureteric stricture** after ureteroscopy or surgery

### Prognosis
- **Non-muscle invasive (Ta, T1):** 5-year survival ~70-90% if treated; high recurrence rate, but progression to muscle-invasive only ~15-20%
- **Muscle-invasive (T2-4):** 5-year survival ~40-60% after curative therapy (cystectomy + chemo); metastatic disease median survival ~14-15 months

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always send urine for culture before treating haematuria as UTI** – if culture negative, consider tumour and refer on 2-week wait pathway
- **Offer smoking cessation** – single strongest modifiable risk factor; provide referral to local stop-smoking service
- **Use a 3-way catheter early** if any clot retention – 2-way catheters block quickly; 22 Fr or larger, irrigation at 20-30 mL/min

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not prescribe NSAIDs** (ibuprofen, diclofenac) for pain in patient with visible haematuria – risk of worsening bleeding and precipitating AKI
- **Do not discharge a patient with visible haematuria** without arranging urgent referral (if >45 years) – even if urine culture positive, treat first then re-check haematuria
- **Do not omit VTE prophylaxis** in admitted patient – but hold if active uncontrolled haemorrhage (discuss with urology). Resume once bleeding controlled

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Visible haematuria worsens or becomes heavy (clots, unable to void)
  - New inability to pass urine / suprapubic pain
  - Fever + rigors (suggests UTI or sepsis)
- **Follow-up:**
  - 2-week wait urology clinic if not yet seen
  - After TURBT: clinical nurse specialist phone follow-up within 48h; flexi cystoscopy 3 months
  - For metastases: oncology clinic within 2 weeks

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call urology registrar if:**
  - Clot retention not clearing with continuous irrigation
  - Haematuria requiring >2 units PRBC / ongoing haemodynamic instability
  - Severe suprapubic pain (suspect perforation)
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥ 6 or single parameter: SBP <90 / HR >120 / RR >25 / SpO2 <90% or GCS ≤13
  - Lactate > 4 mmol/L, anuria > 6 hours despite fluids
  - Anticipated massive transfusion (activate major haemorrhage protocol)
